title: Otso Warakin Gets New Colors for 2026
content:
2026 sees the Otso Cycles Warakin “all road” get two new colors…

Hailey reviewed the Warakin in 2024. Otso markets it as a versatile steel all-road bike. It is designed for multi-surface rides, overnighters, gravel races, and commuting. Frame features include adjustable geometry, lots of mounts for cargo and fenders, clearance for 50 mm tires, suspension-corrected geometry to run a suspension fork, and can be built with gears or as a single-speed.

The two new colorways have just been added to this bike, in Sage and Lavender (below).

Warakin Quick Hits

  • 50 mm tire clearance
  • Suspension corrected for gravel forks
  • New colors (above)
  • Size 49 cm updated
  • Geometry tweaks
  • Additional mounting options

Warakin 2026 Geometry

The Warakin features the same geometry as the Warakin Stainless and Warakin Ti, just with a different frame material. A 56cm Warakin frame has a head tube angle of 71.5º , a seat tube angle of 73º, a reach of 383mm and a stack of 596mm. The Warakin geometry has suspension correction – like all Otso bikes – which allows the rider to install a gravel suspension fork without significantly altering the geometry of the bike. The carbon stock, paint-matched fork has an axle-to-crown of 420mm. All of this contributes to a progressive geometry that will comfortably and confidently carry the rider along the road ahead, no matter the terrain.

Sizes 52, 54, 56, 58, and 60cm follow the same geometry of the Warakin Ti and Warakin Stainless. The 49cm frame has been newly designed for an optimized riding experience for shorter riders. This size was developed with 650b wheels in mind, which reduces toe overlap. Frame tire clearance is still 50mm as it is with the other sizes, and all mounts for cargo, rear rack, and fenders are still present on the new 49cm frame design.

Frame Updates

Beyond the geometry, the frame and fork have design features with versatility in mind. Bottle bosses can be found on the seat tube and triple mounts can be found on the top and underside of the down tube, as well as each side of the fork. The frame is compatible with a rear rack. Fenders for the front and rear wheels can be installed using the mounts on the frame and fork. All cables are externally routed on the underside of the down tube and chain stays; this keeps them out of the way of frame bags, but still easy to access for service. The Warakin is ready to be equipped, no matter how you like to configure your cargo and accessories.

Two new colorways are available now for the Warakin: Sage and Lavender. The paint scheme is simple and clean, giving a nod to the original Warakin Stainless design that helped launch the Otso Cycles brand in 2016. The Sage colorway features a green-gray paint with pearl added in the clear coat. The brown decals and gold stripe perfectly complement the Sage paint, and pair nicely with Wolf Tooth anodized accent components in gold, orange, and espresso. The Lavender colorway takes things in a cool direction, with an easy tint of purple and blue-green decals.

Like all Otso bikes, the new Warakin can be built to order via the Otso Custom Bike Builder. This allows customers to choose everything from drivetrain, wheel size, tires, handlebar width, stem length, and more. The nine color accent options of Wolf Tooth anodized components are especially fun to build a unique bike. Pair the Tangerine colorway with purple accents, Racing Green with espresso, Navy Gold with orange, or any other combination. Otso bikes are assembled in the same building as the Wolf Tooth machine shop.

Purchasing a 2026 Warakin

The Warakin can be purchased as a frameset or as a complete build. Pricing for the frameset starts at $1,950 USD and includes frame, fork, Tuning Chip hardware, axles, seatpost clamp, and bottle cage bolts. Pricing for a complete bike built with Shimano GRX 12-speed at $2,950. Upgrades are available for all build options via the Otso Custom Bike Builder. The new Warakin is available now on OtsoCycles.com and at any bike shop that stocks Otso.

The post Otso Warakin Gets New Colors for 2026 appeared first on The Radavist.
author: John Watson
